Log retention is a forensics problem as much as a compliance one. If your logs only go back 30 days and the attacker was in your network for 90, your investigation starts dead in the water.  #DigitalForensics #DFIR #InformationSecurity

macOS FSEvents is your file system's memory — a detailed journal of every creation, modification, deletion, and permission change on a volume.  FSEvents lives in /.fseventsd/ on each mounted volume. These gzip-compressed binary files persist across reboots and provide a chronological record of file system activity. During an incident, this is gold: you can trace which files an attacker created, modified, or deleted, even if those files no longer exist on disk.  Parse FSEvents with FSEventsParser, mac_apt, or Plaso to reconstruct timelines. Look for suspicious executables dropped in /tmp, LaunchAgents created for persistence, or bulk file deletions indicating anti-forensics (MITRE T1070). FSEvents won't tell you *who* made the change, but it will show you *what* changed and *when* — essential context when building your incident narrative.  Every macOS volume keeps its own FSEvents log. If you're imaging a system with multiple APFS containers or external drives, pull /.fseventsd/ from each one.  #DFIR #macOSForensics

sample_texts: 2 / 2 드디어 공식화된 부정선거    2025년 12월 9일, 미국 조지아주 선거관리위원회(SEB) 청문회에서는 공식적으로 부정선거를 인정했다. 이날 선거관리위원회를 대리한 법

sample_tweets.text: 2 / 2 드디어 공식화된 부정선거    2025년 12월 9일, 미국 조지아주 선거관리위원회(SEB) 청문회에서는 공식적으로 부정선거를 인정했다. 이날 선거관리위원회를 대리한 법률대리인의 발언은 그동안 ‘음모론’으로 치부되던 부정선거 의혹이 공식적인 절차 속에서 확인된 중요한 전환점이었다. 이 소식이 알려지자 그동안 선거 부정을 주장해 왔던 일론 머스크는 자신의 X 계정에 “대규모 투표 사기가 밝혀졌다(Massive voter fraud exposed)“는 글을 올렸다. 반면 이러한 청문회 내용을 주류 언론은 다루지 않았다.   청문회에서는 티나 피터스 측의 디지털 포렌식 전문가 마크 쿡(Mark Cook)이 도미니언 투표 시스템을 대상으로 한 시연을 진행했다. 그는 시스템 내부에 이른바 ’백도어(Backdoor)’가 존재를 폭로하며, 이를 이용해 데이터가 변경되는 모습을 실시간으로 시연했다. 키보드를 조작하는 것만으로도 수천 수만의 표가 순식간에 변경되는 모습은 참석자들에게 큰 충격을 안겨주었다.  FBI  압수수색   이후 2026년 1월 말에는 조지아주 풀턴 카운티 선거본부에 대한 수색영장이 집행되었다. 당시 현장에는 국가정보국(DNI) 국장 툴시 개버드가 모습을 드러냈으며, 수사기관이 투표용지 상자와 컴퓨터 등을 압수한 후 장면이 공개되었다. 이후 수 많은 자료들은 여러 트럭에 실려서 FBI 센터로 옮겨 갔다.  한편 애리조나주 마리코파 카운티의 2020년 선거에 대해서도 여러 차례 감사가 진행됐다. 일부 감사 보고서는 삭제된 파일, 인터넷 접속 기록, 부팅 가능한 드라이브, 타주 유권자 데이터, 익명 사용자 계정의 접속 기록, 외부 IP를 통한 원격 접속 흔적, 감사 직전에 삭제된 선거 데이터와 로그 등이 발견됐다.   거대 부정이 있었다는 것은 투표율이 말해주었다. 2000년부터 2016년까지 미국 대통령 선거의 등록 유권자 기준 평균 투표율은 약 53~54% 수준이었다. 주류 언론이 2020년에 18세 이상 성인 인구를 기준으로 약 60%의 투표율을 제시했다. 그러나 전문가들이 법적으로 투표할 수 없는 비시민권자, 투표할 수 없는 중범죄자, 투표할 수 없는 정신적 무능력자등을 빼자 등록 유권자를 기준으로 계산한 투표율이 약 95%에 달했다. 부정선거 없이는 있을 수 없는 일이 발생한 것이었다.   미국에서 확인된 부정선거는 한국의 선거를 바라보는 시각에도 적지 않은 영향을 미쳤다. 세계 민주주의의 상징으로 여겨지던 미국에서 부정선거가 일어났다는 사실은 한국에서 제기된 각종 ‘음모론’이 근거 없는 주장이 아니라 사실이라는 인식을 확산시켰다.

Suspected Drug Dealer Arrested After Kimberley Police Seize Khat Worth Nearly R4,000 and Cash During CBD Stop-and-Search  Police arrested a 28-year-old suspected drug dealer after discovering Khat with an estimated street value of R3,990 during a patrol in the Kimberley CBD on Friday night. The arrest followed a stop-and-search operation carried out by members attached to the Provincial Operational Response Services, the Frances Baard District Task Team and the Kimberley Flying Squad.  The incident occurred at approximately 22:00 on 10 July 2026 while officers were conducting high-visibility patrols under Operation Shanela II. Members noticed a Toyota Yaris, stopped the vehicle and engaged with the driver. After obtaining his consent, police searched the vehicle and uncovered the suspected Khat along with a substantial amount of cash believed to be the proceeds of criminal activities.  The 28-year-old male suspect was arrested and charged accordingly. Police said he was later transported to a hospital for medical treatment after he reportedly ingested some of the suspected drugs. The seized Khat has an estimated street value of R3,990, while the cash recovered is believed to be linked to criminal activity.  The South African Police Service said operations conducted under the auspices of Operation Shanela II will continue across the province as officers remain committed to addressing serious and violent crime. The suspect remains in police custody following his arrest, while the confiscated drugs and cash form part of the case.

🇬🇧 MOSQUE EFFIGY BURNED – POLICE INVESTIGATE HATE CRIME  A wooden pyre topped with a replica mosque effigy was built in Moygashel, County Tyrone, Northern Ireland, ahead of the traditional Eleventh Night loyalist bonfire, as part of the Twelfth of July commemorations.  The mosque had signs reading “Secure our borders” and “End the threat of radical Islam.” In the window was an effigy of a figure, resembling an Islamic State terrorist holding a knife.  Police launched an investigation into a hate-motivated incident. A 56-year-old man was arrested on suspicion of displaying threatening, abusive, or insulting material intended to stir up hatred under Article 9 of the Public Order NI Order 1987. He remained in custody.   Authorities sent contractors to dismantle the effigy and structure to prevent it from being burned. In response, the organisers lit the bonfire early tonight instead of planned celebrations tomorrow, before the workers arrived. Locals came and celebrated with fireworks as the pyre burned.   Amnesty International’s Northern Ireland director, Patrick Corrigan, described the display as “a blatant attempt to stir up anti-Muslim hatred and intimidate local families,” calling it incitement to hatred.   Crazy

Governance Library Hygiene: Keeping the Control System Current, Owned, and Usable @RialoHQ   A governance system is only as reliable as its library.  The library is where the operating memory lives:  playbooks alerts dashboards SDK components support scripts contract clauses policy mappings audit schemas partner notices certification tests restriction bundles  At first, this library feels manageable.  There are only a few controls. Everyone knows where things are. The same people wrote the playbooks. The same teams remember why each dashboard exists. Then the system grows. More partners. More incidents. More policy states. More exceptions. More SDK versions. More support paths. More contract variants. More dashboards. More alerts. Eventually, the library becomes a risk surface.  Not because the artifacts are bad, but because nobody can tell:  which artifact is current who owns it when it was last reviewed what replaced it which partners depend on it whether it still matches policy whether it should be retired That is why governance library hygiene matters. It is the discipline of keeping the control library current, owned, versioned, reviewed, and safe to use.  Without hygiene, governance memory turns into governance clutter.  1) The library is part of the control plane  A governance library is not documentation. Documentation explains the system. The governance library operates the system. A playbook tells teams how to respond. An alert decides when someone is woken up. A support script shapes what users hear. An SDK component shapes partner behavior. A contract clause shapes enforcement rights. A dashboard shapes decisions. A certification test shapes launch readiness. These artifacts affect real outcomes. So they need the same discipline as production systems: ownership  version control  review cadence  dependency mapping  change approval  archival rules  retirement process  auditability  A stale playbook can be as dangerous as stale code.  A wrong support script can create policy drift. A noisy alert can weaken incident response. A duplicate dashboard can split decision-making. Governance artifacts are operational assets. Treat them that way. 2) The core rule: every artifact needs an owner Unowned artifacts become stale.  Every governance artifact should have:  primary owner  backup owner owning team review cadence escalation path replacement owner if the team changes Ownership should be visible inside the artifact itself. Bad: “Owned by partner ops.” Better: “Primary owner: Partner Reliability Lead. Backup: Control Plane PM. Review cadence: quarterly.”  The owner is responsible for:  keeping the artifact current  reviewing effectiveness  responding to retirement signals  updating dependencies  approving changes  ensuring audit history is preserved  Ownership does not mean one person does all the work.  It means one person is accountable for the artifact not decaying.  3) Classify artifacts by type  The library should not be one flat folder. Different artifact types need different rules. A practical taxonomy: Response artifacts playbooks restriction bundles  incident communication templates  re-entry checklists probation rules  Purpose: Guide operational response. Detection artifacts  alerts  dashboards monitoring thresholds anomaly rules partner scorecard metrics Purpose: Detect and prioritize risk. Implementation artifacts  SDK components  API wrappers  reference UI  certification tests  integration guides  Purpose: Make correct partner behavior easier.  Communication artifacts  support scripts  partner notices user-facing policy copy status fragments escalation language Purpose: Keep explanations safe and consistent.  Contractual artifacts SLA clauses  partner obligations  audit rights deprecation terms override rules Purpose: Make expectations enforceable. Evidence artifacts  audit schema closure checklist  evidence templates  review records effectiveness cards Purpose: Make decisions reconstructable. Each type needs its own review cadence and retirement rules. Do not manage support scripts the same way you manage contract clauses.  4) Use lifecycle states Every artifact should have a lifecycle state.  Recommended states:  Draft Being created. Not approved for operational use. Active Current and approved for use. Shadow  Running in observation mode. Not yet authoritative. Deprecated  Still usable in limited cases, but replacement exists.  Retired No longer active. Preserved for audit history. Archived Historical reference only. Not used in operations. Superseded  Replaced by a newer artifact. These states prevent confusion.  A playbook should not be “kind of old.” An SDK component should not be “probably still supported.” A dashboard should not be “maybe not the source of truth.”  The state should be explicit. 5) Version everything that affects decisions If an artifact can affect a decision, it needs versioning. That includes: playbooks  restriction bundles support scripts  contract clauses  policy mappings  reason-code contracts  partner notices  audit schemas  SDK certification tests  Versioning matters because future reviewers need to know what rule existed at the time of the decision.  Example:  A partner was restricted on March 3.  Which playbook version applied? Which reason-code mapping was active? Which partner notice template was used? Which support script did agents see? Which SLA clause governed the partner?  Without versioning, audits become memory exercises.  Versioning turns governance into evidence.  6) Every artifact should declare its dependencies  Artifacts rarely stand alone.  A playbook may depend on:  alert trigger  restriction bundle  partner notice template  support script  retest matrix  audit fields  An SDK component may depend on: reason-code contract API behavior  policy state model  certification test  partner migration guide  A contract clause may depend on:  scorecard metric  enforcement trigger audit evidence partner notice process Each artifact should list: upstream dependencies downstream dependencies replacement artifact, if any related failure classes related partner surfaces  This prevents accidental breakage.  If you retire an alert, you should know which playbooks used it.  If you simplify a policy state, you should know which support scripts and reason-code mappings need updates.  Dependency mapping is what makes safe cleanup possible. 7) Build an artifact index A governance library needs an index. Not a folder tree. An index.  For each artifact, track:  artifact ID  name  type  owner  lifecycle state  version original purpose linked failure class linked policy area linked partners or partner class last reviewed date  next review date  last effectiveness review retirement status replacement artifact dependencies audit references  This index becomes the control library’s source of truth.  Teams should not have to search five folders and three Slack threads to know which artifact is current. 8) Review cadence by artifact type Not every artifact needs the same review frequency. Monthly review  Best for:  noisy alerts active incident dashboards high-risk support scripts open restriction bundles active migration trackers  Reason: These change quickly and can create immediate confusion.  Quarterly review  Best for:  playbooks  partner scorecard metrics  certification tests  reason-code mappings  audit schema fields  partner notice templates  Reason: These shape recurring operations and should stay aligned with actual incidents.  Semiannual review Best for: SDK components contract clauses policy modules partner onboarding requirements governance workflows Reason: These are heavier to change but must not drift indefinitely. Event-triggered review Required after: major incident policy epoch change partner restriction deprecation completion SDK major release contract template update repeated manual handling spike  support disclosure issue  A calendar cadence is not enough.  The library should also respond to events.  9) Review should produce decisions  A review is not complete unless it produces a decision.  Allowed decisions:  keep  tune expand merge replace retire  archive risk accept  Bad review outcome: “Looks fine.”  Better: “Keep active. Next review in 90 days. No dependency changes.”  Bad review outcome: “Needs cleanup.” Better: “Replace with PLAYBOOK-014 by August 15, then archive PLAYBOOK-006 after dependency check.” Governance library hygiene depends on decision discipline. Otherwise reviews become rituals. 10) Define freshness requirements Every active artifact should have freshness requirements.  Examples: Playbooks  Must be reviewed after:  any activation  any false positive any serious missed trigger any policy state change any restriction ladder change Alerts  Must be reviewed if: false positives exceed threshold  no action taken in 90 days  owner changes  traffic baseline changes related playbook changes Support scripts Must be reviewed if: reason-code mapping changes policy copy changes disclosure boundary changes support escalation rises  partner UI changes  SDK components  Must be reviewed if:  API behavior changes  policy state model changes  new high-risk flow launches  partner adoption stalls certification failures cluster Freshness is not about time alone. It is about whether the artifact still matches the operating reality. 11) Keep current and historical artifacts separate One common failure is mixing active and archived artifacts in the same search path. During incidents, people search quickly. If old playbooks, old scripts, and old dashboard links appear beside current ones, mistakes happen.  Use clear separation: current library  deprecated library retired archive  historical audit references Active artifacts should be easy to find.  Retired artifacts should be easy to verify, but hard to accidentally use. That is the balance.  12) Make retired artifacts visibly retired  A retired artifact should show:  retired date  retired by  reason  replacement artifact  active dates  audit preservation note  do-not-use label Do not rely on folder location alone. People copy links. Old bookmarks survive. Partner packets get reused. Support teams search old pages. The artifact itself should clearly say it is retired. This prevents zombie artifacts.  13) Maintain a single source of truth  For each artifact category, define the authoritative location.  Examples: active playbooks live in the control plane library support scripts live in support tooling, not copied docs SDK requirements live in certification registry contract clauses live in legal template repository  dashboard source of truth lives in scorecard index  policy mappings live in reason-code contract registry  Copies are dangerous. If a support script appears in a Google Doc, a partner packet, a wiki, and support tooling, one of them will eventually go stale. The library should link to source artifacts, not duplicate them. 14) Use artifact IDs Names change.  IDs should not.  Examples:  PB-ENF-004: Enforcement parity playbook ALERT-RET-002: Duplicate refresh job alert SDK-POS-003: Posture display component SUP-DISC-005: Disclosure-safe support script SLA-DEP-002: Hard-date deprecation clause  TEST-RC-006: Reason-code mapping certification test  IDs make audit trails reliable.  They also make dependency mapping easier.  An incident record should reference artifact IDs and versions, not vague names.  15) Track artifact effectiveness  The library should not only track existence.  It should track usefulness.  For each major artifact, maintain:  adoption last activation false-positive rate recurrence impact  manual handling impact  partner compliance impact  support escalation impact  review decision  This prevents artifact hoarding.  A playbook that never reduces manual handling should be tuned or replaced.  An alert that never produces action should be retired or rewritten. An SDK component that partners avoid should be improved or made required where justified. Library hygiene is not about cataloging artifacts. It is about keeping useful artifacts active. 16) Link artifacts to failure classes Every artifact should answer: Which failure class does this protect against?  Examples:  duplicate refresh jobs partner-only allow stale proof authorization support disclosure drift  posture missing in partner UI  reason-code collapse  unsafe re-entry  override abuse  deprecation slippage  If an artifact cannot be tied to a failure class, policy requirement, or compliance obligation, review whether it should exist.  This keeps the library grounded in actual risk. 17) Track artifact coverage For each major failure class, track whether the library has coverage across the control loop. Example: retry pressure Coverage: alert: yes  playbook: yes  restriction bundle: partial  SDK wrapper: yes  support script: yes  partner SLA clause: yes  retest matrix: yes  audit field: yes  effectiveness review: pending  This view shows gaps and clutter. A failure class with no playbook may have closure debt. A failure class with three alerts and two dashboards may have retirement debt. Coverage mapping helps balance both. 18) Manage version compatibility Versioning is especially important for SDKs, contracts, and policy mappings.  The library should know:  which partners use which SDK version  which partners are under which contract version  which policy epoch is active  which support scripts map to which reason-code version  which certification tests apply to which partner class  Without compatibility tracking, governance becomes inconsistent.  One partner may be judged by a new standard while another operates under an old one.  Sometimes that is valid. But the system should know it. Hidden version differences create enforcement surprises. 19) Avoid artifact duplication Duplication is one of the fastest ways to create library drift. Examples: two dashboards for the same metric two support scripts for the same state  two playbooks for overlapping failure classes  two SDK guides with different requirements  two contract clauses describing the same obligation differently  Duplication should trigger one of three decisions:  merge  designate source of truth  retire one version  Do not leave duplicate artifacts active unless the difference is intentional and clearly explained.  20) Library hygiene and partner onboarding  Partners experience governance through the artifacts they receive.  If the library is messy, onboarding becomes messy.  Partner-facing artifacts should be especially clean: current SDK requirement current certification tests current support script package current reason-code mapping current deprecation obligations current posture behavior current escalation path A partner should not need to ask: “Which version is the real one?”  If they do, internal library hygiene has already failed externally.  21) Library hygiene and incident response  During incidents, teams do not have time to interpret the library.  The current path must be obvious.  For every known failure class, incident responders should quickly find:  active playbook  alert source  owner restriction bundle partner notice support script retest path audit requirements escalation rule If responders must search and compare, library hygiene is weak. A good library reduces response time.  A bad library creates response ambiguity.  22) Library hygiene and audits  Auditors need history.  Operators need current truth.  The library must serve both.  That means:  active artifacts are clearly current  retired artifacts remain accessible  old versions are preserved incident records link to artifact versions replacements are recorded retirement reasons are documented Do not delete history to simplify operations.  Do not expose history as if it were current.  The library needs both memory and clarity.  23) The library hygiene dashboard  A useful hygiene dashboard tracks:  artifacts by lifecycle state  artifacts overdue for review  artifacts without owner  artifacts without linked failure class  active artifacts with no recent use  alerts with high false-positive rate dashboards without decision owner support scripts tied to retired policy states SDK components past sunset duplicate artifacts by category  retired artifacts still referenced by active workflows  The most important metric: Unowned active artifacts. If an artifact is active and unowned, it is already decaying.  24) Library hygiene review meeting  A monthly hygiene review can be short.  Agenda:  Which active artifacts are unowned?  Which artifacts are overdue for review?  Which artifacts caused confusion in recent incidents?  Which artifacts have poor effectiveness? Which artifacts should be merged, replaced, or retired? Which policy or SDK changes require dependency updates? Which partner-facing artifacts need refresh? The meeting should produce changes to the library, not just a list of concerns. 25) Library hygiene anti-patterns “The wiki is the library”  A wiki can document artifacts, but it is rarely enough to govern them. “Everyone knows this is old”  If it is not marked retired, someone will eventually use it.  “We will update copies later”  Copies create drift.  “No owner needed”  No owner means no maintenance.  “Review means someone glanced at it”  Review should produce a decision. “Archived means deleted” Archive means preserved but inactive. “The newest artifact is automatically the source of truth” Only if dependencies, links, and old references are updated. “Partners can figure out the right version” They should not have to. 26) The minimum viable governance library A small team does not need a huge system.  But it does need minimum hygiene.  At minimum, track:  artifact name  type  owner  state  version  last reviewed  next review  linked failure class replacement, if any dependency notes That is enough to prevent most chaos. As the system grows, add: effectiveness metrics  partner compatibility  retirement debt  closure debt  dependency graph  audit version links  Start lightweight.  But start early. Library hygiene is much easier before the library becomes messy. 27) The real standard A governance library is healthy when operators can trust it. They can see what is current. They can find the right playbook quickly. They know who owns each artifact. They know which support script is approved. They know which SDK version is required. They know which dashboard is authoritative. They know which contract clause applies. They know what has been retired and what replaced it. That is hygiene. Not a beautiful folder structure. Not a long wiki. Not a catalog nobody maintains. A healthy library makes the control system easier to operate. It preserves memory without creating clutter.  It keeps current artifacts visible and old artifacts safe.  It turns governance knowledge into usable infrastructure.  Because governance does not fail only when rules are missing.  It also fails when the rules are scattered, stale, duplicated, unowned, or impossible to trust.  Library hygiene is how the system keeps its own memory clean.

Today we're turning our attention to the next phase of the Nodus Protocol roadmap, and it's a significant one: decentralized governance and an open developer ecosystem.  First, DAO Governance. Security decisions shouldn't sit with a single team. We're building an on-chain voting and proposal system so the community can directly shape how the protocol evolves, from how threats are verified to how the network is funded and prioritized. Guardians who protect the ecosystem should also have a voice in governing it.  Second, a public Nodus API and developer SDK. The threat intelligence powering Nodus Shield shouldn't stay locked inside one extension. We want any wallet, dApp, or marketplace on Solana to be able to integrate real-time protection with a few lines of code. Our goal is to make "protect before you connect" a standard across the entire ecosystem, not just a single product.  Both are early in development, and we'll be sharing progress as they take shape. This is how Nodus grows from an app into infrastructure.  $NODUS $Solana

That threat was deemed not to be entirely credible, according to some of those officials.   But the information, combined with Trump’s proximity to Iran as hostilities were resuming, was enough that the Secret Service, the White House’s military office and some national security staff determined Trump’s preferred plane—a jet gifted by the Qatari government and overhauled by the U.S. Air Force—wasn’t adequate to fly the president home, the officials said. @wsj Read more: on.wsj.com/4yeiZv1

Africa Must Lead Response to Growing Security Threats, Buratai Tells Forum  By Zagazola Makama  Former Chief of Army Staff, retired Lt.-Gen. Tukur Yusuf Buratai, has called for African-led solutions to the continent’s worsening security challenges, warning that the continent is facing an unprecedented convergence of threats requiring collective action.  Buratai made the call on Friday while delivering the keynote address titled, “Africa’s Priority Security Threats,” at the 32nd Parliamentary Intelligence-Security Forum in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia.  The retired army chief identified terrorism and violent extremism, transnational organised crime, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, governance-related instability, and the erosion of sovereignty through external dependence as the five major security threats confronting Africa.  According to him, the continent can no longer rely solely on external interventions but must strengthen regional cooperation, intelligence sharing and indigenous security capabilities.  Buratai described the Sahel as the global epicentre of terrorism, noting that the region now accounts for nearly half of all terror-related deaths worldwide.  He said that between January and November 2025, West Africa recorded about 450 terrorist attacks, resulting in more than 1,900 fatalities.  The former army chief noted that although Burkina Faso had recorded significant military gains under President Ibrahim Traoré, with government forces reclaiming about 72.7 per cent of occupied territories by June 2025 through Operation Lalmassga, extremist groups continued to stage deadly attacks.  He said intensified military operations in early 2026 reportedly eliminated at least 400 militants, but fresh attacks by jihadist groups, including those linked to Jama’at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), underscored the persistent threat.  Buratai also expressed concern over the withdrawal of Burkina Faso, Mali and Niger from the Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS), saying the development had weakened regional intelligence-sharing and collective security efforts.  He warned that violent extremism was spreading beyond the Sahel into coastal West African countries, citing a sharp increase in attacks in northern Benin over the past three years.  On transnational organised crime, Buratai said West Africa was facing increasingly interconnected threats involving terrorism financing, piracy, arms trafficking, drug smuggling and cybercrime.  He noted that criminal networks were becoming more sophisticated and exploiting weak border controls, digital technologies and fragile governance structures to expand their operations across the region.  The retired general also identified cybersecurity as an emerging national security challenge, stressing that African countries must invest in cyber defence capabilities, digital infrastructure protection and regional cooperation to counter growing cyber threats.  Buratai urged African governments, policymakers and security institutions to adopt coordinated, home-grown strategies capable of addressing the continent’s evolving security landscape while safeguarding sovereignty and promoting sustainable peace and development.  He emphasised that lasting security on the continent would require stronger institutions, enhanced intelligence cooperation, economic resilience and greater political commitment among African nations.

“We’re Only Chief Security Officers in Name!” — Governor Lawal Reveals Why Nigeria’s Security Crisis Won’t End Without State Police❤️🔥👇  Zamfara State Governor, Dauda Lawal, has declared that Nigeria’s worsening insecurity cannot be effectively tackled unless governors are given full authority through the establishment of state police.  Speaking at the Arise News Town Hall Summit on Building a National Consensus for State Police & National Security held at the ThisDay Dome in Abuja, Governor Lawal argued that governors are currently called “chief security officers” of their states only in title, as they lack command and control over security agencies.  “We’re being called chief security officers of our respective states; however, the command and control structure is not under the governors,” Lawal said.  According to the governor, creating state police would empower governors to take direct responsibility for securing lives and property, improve accountability, and strengthen the fight against crime through better-equipped and more locally responsive police forces.  “I am happy with the recent development of state police because it allows us to keep to our responsibilities and for our people also to hold us accountable as far as the security situation is concerned in our respective states,” he stated.  Lawal expressed confidence that state police would significantly reduce insecurity across the country.  “I strongly believe that, by the time we have state police, it will go a long way in solving a lot of the challenges, and we can then bear our names as proper chief security officers of our respective states.”  Addressing fears that governors could misuse state-controlled police against political opponents, Lawal dismissed the concerns, pointing out that many states already operate local security outfits without using them to suppress opposition.  He, however, urged lawmakers to establish strong legal safeguards to prevent abuse.  “I support state police. I recognise people’s fears of possible abuse, but with the right processes in place, those fears will be allayed.”  The summit brought together governors, lawmakers, security chiefs, traditional rulers, civil society organisations, and policy experts to deliberate on state policing and broader national security reforms amid growing concerns over insecurity across Nigeria.

If you operate in the region, it’s worth the read: threatmon.io/middle-east-cyb… #CyberSecurity #ThreatIntelligence #MiddleEast #Ransomware #APT #DarkWeb #CTI

⚠️ 11 Million US Resumes and PII Allegedly Leaked on Darkweb Forum  A darkweb forum post claims the exposure of a massive dataset containing 11 million detailed resumes from US job seekers, totaling 1TB of data. The actor alleges that the collection spans over 20 years and includes sensitive personally identifiable information (PII) such as email addresses, phone numbers, and full home addresses. While the specific source of the breach is not identified in the listing, the actor offers a sample preview to demonstrate data quality before directing interested parties to contact them via Telegram.  According to the post, the dataset includes a metadata file (meta.csv) with the following fields:  - sjt_user_id: Unique seeker ID - register_date: Account registration date - firstname: First name - lastname: Last name - email: Primary email address - phone: Primary phone number - address: Street address - city: City - state: State/province code - zip: ZIP/postal code - country: Country code - dockey: Resume document key (MD5) - original_name: Original uploaded resume filename - resume_size: File size in bytes - create_date: Resume creation timestamp - modified_date: Last resume update timestamp - saved_filename: Decoded resume filename inside files/ (PDF/DOCX/DOC)  The sample preview provided in the thread lists three records with names, emails, phone numbers, cities, states, ZIP codes, and associated resume filenames in DOCX format. If authentic, this leak could facilitate large-scale identity theft, targeted phishing campaigns, and social engineering attacks against individuals across the United States.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified, nor has the original source of the data been confirmed.  #DataBreach #PII #IdentityTheft #DarkWeb #USPrivacy #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity

🚨 Threat Actor Claims Massive FortiGate Credential Leak Affecting Spotify, Samsung, and Oracle  A darkweb forum post reveals the alleged sale of a large dataset containing credentials tied to FortiGate SSL-VPN portals and administrative access points. The actor claims the dataset, labeled as "FortiBleed dataset 2026," contains 68,520 total credential records spanning 21,387 distinct domains. According to the listing, the data includes masked login URLs, corporate email addresses (FortiGuardID), and the country of access for each record.  The post alleges that credentials for several major global technology and enterprise organizations are included in the leak. The top affected domains by estimated user count listed in the sample include:  *   spotify.com – Music Streaming – 4,223,908 users *   samsung.com – Consumer Electronics – 2,325,467 users *   spe.sony.com – Entertainment Media – 1,958,292 users *   mihoyo.com – Video Games – 723,402 users *   oracle.com – Enterprise Software – 704,733 users *   mercadolivre.com – E-commerce Marketplace – 694,280 users *   service.alibaba.com – E-commerce Marketplace – 611,722 users *   huawei.com – Telecommunications Equipment – 478,509 users *   epfindia.gov.in – Government Services – 339,113 users *   lenovo.com – Computer Hardware – 272,000 users  The seller claims that 400 to 500 of the accessed credentials remain valid. The geographic distribution of the records reportedly shows significant activity from India (9,371 records), the USA (6,151 records), Taiwan (3,419 records), Mexico (3,149 records), and Turkey (2,894 records). The file size is listed as 8.68 MB.  If authentic, exposure of valid FortiGate VPN credentials could enable unauthorized remote access to internal corporate networks, potentially leading to lateral movement and data exfiltration within these high-value targets. At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by the affected organizations or security researchers.

🚨 Threat Actor Claims Breach of Keccel Billing Database Exposing 3.4M Congolese Numbers  A darkweb forum post reveals that a threat actor has allegedly obtained full access to the billing database of Keccel Technologies S.A.R.L, a fintech and SMS gateway provider based in Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of Congo. The actor claims to have compromised the company's entire billing and event infrastructure, exposing data related to 29 corporate clients who route A2P SMS and WhatsApp messages through major Congolese operators.  According to the listing, the breach exposes significant volumes of communication metadata and subscriber information:  *   **3,424,158** unique phone numbers from the DRC (+243) *   **35,698,321** total SMS events *   **26,428,316** billable SMS messages  The post details the distribution of this traffic across mobile network operators, with Airtel accounting for 39.4% of phones (1,348,955) and Vodacom holding 26.0% (890,293). Orange and Africell follow with 24.0% and 8.7% respectively. The actor also lists top sender brands utilizing the gateway, including gambling platforms like Winner Bet and betPawa, financial services such as Visa Pay, and technology firms like Altech and Africa's Talking.  Sample data provided in the thread includes CSV exports containing customer IDs, organization names, creation timestamps, and billing identifiers for entities such as KECCEL, NETMOBILE, DIGIT MARKET, and MTARGET FRANCE. Additionally, the post displays logs linking specific phone numbers to sender brands and message counts, potentially revealing user engagement patterns for services ranging from banking alerts to betting notifications.  Keccel Technologies operates as a critical infrastructure provider for digital communications in the region, facilitating transactions for banks, utilities, and government bodies. If authentic, the exposure of this billing data could enable targeted phishing campaigns against corporate clients or facilitate fraud by revealing active user bases for high-value services. At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified.

sample_tweets.text: 🐧 The Linux Foundation has launched Akrites, a new industry initiative focused on protecting critical open source software from AI-enabled cyber threats.  The initiative brings together major technology and security organizations to create a trusted framework for coordinated vulnerability remediation and responsible disclosure across the open source ecosystem.  * Improves coordination between maintainers, security researchers, and vendors. * Accelerates vulnerability remediation and responsible disclosure. * Aims to strengthen the resilience of critical open source projects against increasingly sophisticated AI-assisted attacks.  Analyst Note: Open source software underpins much of the world's digital infrastructure, but maintainers often lack the resources to respond quickly to emerging threats. As AI lowers the barrier for vulnerability discovery and exploitation, centralized coordination initiatives like Akrites can significantly reduce response times and improve ecosystem-wide resilience.  Source: Linux Foundation linuxfoundation.org/press/li… #DDW #DarkWeb #Intelligence #OpenSource

🇪🇺 EU Extends Temporary "Chat Control" Rules  The European Parliament has backed the return of temporary rules allowing online platforms to voluntarily scan certain user communications for child sexual abuse material (CSAM).  * More MEPs voted against (314) than in favor (276), but opponents fell short of the 361 votes needed to block the measure. * The temporary rules would remain in force until 2028 unless replaced sooner. * Parliament voted to exempt end-to-end encrypted services such as WhatsApp, Signal, and Telegram.  Analyst Note: The vote highlights the ongoing tension between child protection and digital privacy. While supporters view voluntary scanning as an important investigative tool, critics warn that expanding content scanning on non-encrypted platforms could set a broader precedent for online surveillance.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #EU

🪙 Glassnode Customer Database Allegedly Offered for Sale  A threat actor is advertising the alleged sale of a database belonging to crypto analytics platform Glassnode.  According to the forum post, the seller claims the dataset includes:  * Data from approximately 850,000 users * A full HubSpot CRM export * Customer account information * Password hashes * Subscription and plan details * API keys * Marketing consent and referral data * Affiliate and analytics-related fields  The actor states the data was allegedly obtained in May 2026 and is offering a single copy for sale.  Analyst Note: These claims have not been independently verified. If authentic, exposed API keys and customer account metadata could present significant security risks beyond credential exposure. Organizations using Glassnode should monitor for any official disclosure and rotate API credentials where appropriate.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Crypto

🚨 Threat Actor Advertises Fraudulent “Emergency Data Request” Services  A threat actor is offering access to user records from major technology and telecom platforms, allegedly by abusing emergency data request procedures.  According to the forum post, the seller claims they can obtain:  * Telegram-linked phone numbers, recent IP addresses, and timestamps * Records associated with Meta platforms, including Facebook and Instagram * Subscriber data from T-Mobile and AT&T * Information from Microsoft and Pinterest  Prices range from $500 to $1,000 per request, with payment accepted in cryptocurrency.  Analyst Note: The claims have not been independently verified. If genuine, this may indicate abuse of emergency disclosure channels, compromised law-enforcement accounts, forged requests, or insider access. Platforms should review emergency-request authentication controls and audit unusual disclosure activity.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Cybercrime

🚨 NCIC Correctional Visitation Data Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has begun publishing what they claim is data obtained from NCIC, a U.S. provider of communication and video-visitation services for correctional facilities.  The visible sample allegedly contains:  * Correctional facility identifiers * Inmate and visitor connection timestamps * Visit scheduling and session records * Video-recording metadata * Session tokens and internal system identifiers * Visit status and termination details  The forum post labels the release as “Part 1,” suggesting additional material may follow.  Analyst Note: The authenticity and full scope of the alleged leak have not been independently verified. If genuine, visitation records and session metadata could expose highly sensitive information concerning incarcerated individuals, their visitors, and participating correctional facilities.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #USA

🇫🇷 French Aviation Platform Flymoove Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has claimed to have released a database associated with Flymoove, a French SaaS platform focused on business aviation and regional air mobility.  According to the forum post, the alleged data includes:  * Full names * Email addresses and phone numbers * Dates of birth * Residential addresses * Country and postal code information * Passport or identity document numbers * Document types and expiration dates  Analyst Note: The authenticity and scope of the alleged dataset have not been independently verified. If confirmed, the exposure of passport and identity-document data could create serious risks of identity theft, travel-related fraud, account impersonation, and targeted phishing.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #France

🇪🇬 GoBus Database and Internal Documents Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has published what they claim is the complete database of GoBus, a transportation booking platform.  * The alleged SQL database is approximately 16 GB. * The actor claims it contains data belonging to around 1 million users. * Reported fields include email addresses, phone numbers, hashed passwords, and complete ride histories. * An additional 4 GB archive allegedly contains internal documents related to drivers and employees. * The post claims the data was obtained within the past two weeks.  Analyst Note: The authenticity and scope of the alleged leak have not been independently verified. If confirmed, the exposure of ride histories and contact details could create serious privacy, phishing, and account-compromise risks.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #GoBus

🇨🇭 Public Data from Swiss Classifieds Platform Shared on Underground Forum  A forum user has published a dataset allegedly scraped from PetitesAnnonces.ch, a Swiss online classifieds platform.  * The archive reportedly contains 202,961 records in JSON format. * The poster explicitly describes the dataset as scraped public information rather than a compromised private database. * The visible sample includes listing references, advertisement text, phone numbers, account metadata, and category information. * Multiple download mirrors were shared through the forum.  Analyst Note: This appears to be large-scale collection and redistribution of publicly accessible listings, not confirmed unauthorized access to the platform’s internal systems. Even public data can create privacy and abuse risks when aggregated at scale.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #Switzerland

🇫🇷 French Handisport Federation Database Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has claimed to have released a database associated with Handisport.org, the website of the Fédération Française Handisport.  According to the forum post:  * The dataset allegedly contains 59,932 records. * The database is being distributed through underground channels. * The post does not provide enough visible detail to confirm the exact categories of exposed information.  Analyst Note: The authenticity, scope, and origin of the alleged dataset have not been independently verified. Given that the organization supports athletes with disabilities, any exposure of member or participant information could create heightened privacy and targeted social-engineering risks.  #DDW #Intelligence #DarkWeb #France

🇦🇺 HotToner Australia Customer Database Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has published what they claim is a customer database belonging to HotToner.com.au, an Australian online retailer specializing in printer ink, toner, and related consumables.  According to the forum post:  * The dataset allegedly contains more than 201,700 customer records. * Claimed fields include names, email addresses, telephone and fax numbers, company details, IP addresses, newsletter preferences, and customer-group information. * Order records reportedly contain product, quantity, price, shipping, billing, and payment-related details. * Sample records were shared to support the claim.  Analyst Note: The authenticity and scope of the alleged breach have not been independently verified. If confirmed, the exposed order and contact data could be used for targeted phishing, invoice fraud, and account impersonation.  #DDW #DarkWeb #Intelligence #Australia

🇦🇷 Argentina's Supreme Court Allegedly Targeted in Data Leak Claim  A threat actor has claimed to have compromised systems associated with Argentina's Supreme Court (Corte Suprema de Justicia) and is advertising the alleged database on a cybercrime forum.  According to the post, the leaked data allegedly includes:  * Employee IDs * Full names * National document numbers * Tax IDs (CUIL) * Dates of birth * Judicial departments * Job titles * Addresses * Phone numbers * Email addresses  The actor also shared what appears to be sample judicial documentation and claimed additional data would be released via Telegram.  Analyst Note: The authenticity of the claimed breach has not been independently verified. If confirmed, the exposure of judicial personnel information could present significant privacy and security risks. The affected organization should investigate the claim, validate the samples, and determine whether unauthorized access occurred.  #DDW #DarkWeb #Intelligence #Argentina

🇪🇨 Ecuador Ministry of Foreign Affairs Database Allegedly Leaked  A threat actor has claimed to have leaked data associated with Ecuador's Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Cancillería del Ecuador) and is advertising the database on a cybercrime forum.  According to the post, the exposed information allegedly includes:  * Employee names and institutional positions * Organizational units and office locations * Institutional email addresses * Telephone numbers and extensions * Staff distribution records * Monthly and annual salary information * Overtime, bonuses, and additional compensation * Provincial and regional management directories * Personnel responsible for public information requests * Organizational planning and performance data  The actor also shared sample records as proof of the alleged compromise.  Analyst Note: The authenticity of the claimed leak has not been independently verified. If confirmed, the exposure of government employee directories and compensation records could facilitate targeted phishing, social engineering, and intelligence collection against public sector personnel.  #DDW #DarkWeb #Intelligence #Ecuador

sample_tweets.text: The NSA Tailored Access Operations (TAO) was renamed to CNO (Computer Network Operations) but recently reverted the name back to TAO.  very interesting (I have no idea what this means).

Chat, I have to do a weird rant opinion thingy and I hope someone understand what I'm saying.  I've noticed a strange phenomena where people who grew up poor have this weird mentality like, they feel they're not good enough to not be poor? I was "poor" growing up, not poor-poor living in a shack, but money was definitely tight and sometimes dinner sucked.  Anyway, I've met TONS of people who come from lower class families and lives, and who act scared to try to pivot into cybersecurity, or computer-science in general.  I don't know if it's self-esteem, or some weird internal thingie on classism, I don't know.  Maybe I sound crazy. Maybe someone smarter than me can convey the idea better. But it 100% happens. I see genuinely intelligent people, definitely capable of performing the type of work I do, end up doing construction or working a dead-end job somewhere and struggling financially and hating their lives because they're not intellectually stimulated. They could do so much more ... but they don't.

llm_summary:
🤖 [GEMINI 분석] 2026-07-11 18:31 KST
🟠 위협 점수: 8/10 (높음)

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 위협 환경은 AI를 악용한 공격 기법이 고도화되고 있으며, 특히 이미지 내에 프롬프트 인젝션을 숨겨 AI 에이전트를 기만하는 'Ghostcommit'과 같은 새로운 공격 벡터가 등장했습니다 ([BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/ghostcommit-hides-prompt-injection-in-images-to-fool-ai-agents-steal-secrets/)). 또한, 공급망 공격의 일환으로 GitHub 리포지토리 탈취 후 악성 npm 패키지를 배포하는 사례가 지속적으로 보고되고 있으며 ([TheHackerNews](https://2026/07/injective-labs-github-compromise-pushes.html)), CISA의 BOD 26-04 지침에 따라 고위험 취약점에 대한 3일 이내 패치 대응이 강제되는 등 규제 준수 압박이 거세지고 있습니다 ([Qualys Blog](https://blog.qualys.com/category/qualys-insights)).

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **Cavern Manticore**: ([Check Point Research](https://research.checkpoint.com/2026/cavern-manticore-exposing-iran-linked-modular-c2-framework/)) 이란 연계 APT 그룹으로, 이스라엘을 타겟으로 모듈형 C2 프레임워크를 운용 중.
- **Armored Likho**: ([Securelist](https://securelist.com/tr/armored-likho-apt-with-busysnake-stealer/120292/)) 러시아 타겟의 APT 캠페인 수행, AI 생성 로더와 Python 기반 'BusySnake Stealer' 사용.
- **The Gentlemen**: ([Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/the-gentlemen-ransomware/)) 제휴 모델(Affiliate model)을 통해 급성장 중인 신규 랜섬웨어 조직.
- **ShinyHunters**: ([AhnLab ASEC](https://asec.ahnlab.com/ko/94410/)) 북미·유럽 지역의 의료, 보안, 미디어 등 다양한 산업군을 대상으로 대규모 데이터 유출 사고 주도.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **한국사회보장정보원-한국생명존중희망재단**: 보건·복지 데이터 연계 자살예방 협력 체계 구축 ([데일리시큐](https://www.dailysecu.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=207564))
- **클로봇-에이프로**: 사족보행 로봇(Spot)과 AI 융합 산업 안전점검 사업 추진 ([데일리시큐](https://www.dailysecu.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=207563))
- **사물인터넷혁신융합대학사업단**: 디토닉 등 딥테크 기업과 IoT 인재 양성 협약 ([데일리시큐](https://www.dailysecu.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=207562))
- **Kimsuky**: 보안 담당자를 타겟으로 한 스피어 피싱 공격 지속 ([이스트시큐리티 알약](https://blog.alyac.co.kr/5765))

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-33697**: Attested TLS 취약점, 고위험군 ([ResearchGate26](https://researchgate.net/profile/Muh…))
- **CVE-2025-6784**: WordPress Code Engine 플러그인 RCE 취약점 ([CVEnew](https://twitter.com/CVEnew/status/2075871708234301930#m))
- **CVE-2026-14262**: WordPress Simple JWT Login 인증 우회 및 권한 상승 ([infoflowcloud](https://twitter.com/infoflowcloud/status/2075871771215929622#m))
- **CVE-2026-7655**: WordPress SureCart 플러그인 계정 탈취/권한 상승 ([infoflowcloud](https://twitter.com/infoflowcloud/status/2075871725896528268#m))
- **CVE-2026-15335**: WordPress Booking Package 플러그인 SQL Injection ([infoflowcloud](https://twitter.com/infoflowcloud/status/2075871775632568470#m))

### 5. IOC 요약
- **해시**: `MpClient.dll` (Go-compiled fake, [Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/vidar-stealer-xmrig-miner-campaign-analysis/))
- **CVE**: `CVE-2026-33697`, `CVE-2025-6784`, `CVE-2026-14262`, `CVE-2026-7655`, `CVE-2026-15335` (상기 출처 참조)

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
- **공급망 점검**: 사용 중인 npm 패키지 및 오픈소스 라이브러리의 무결성을 검증하고, 특히 GitHub 리포지토리의 비정상적인 커밋/패키지 업데이트 이력을 확인하십시오.
- **Zimbra/ShareFile 패치**: Zimbra Classic Web Client 취약점 및 ShareFile Storage Zone Controllers 관련 보안 권고를 즉시 적용하고, 필요 시 서비스 일시 중단 조치를 취하십시오.
- **AI 보안 가이드라인**: 내부 AI 에이전트 및 코드 리뷰 봇에 대한 프롬프트 인젝션 방어책을 마련하고, 외부 이미지 파일의 자동 처리 프로세스를 제한하십시오.

### 7. 위협 수준 평가
- **점수**: 8/10
- **근거**: 공급망 공격(npm)과 고위험 취약점(RCE/Auth Bypass)이 결합되어 기업 인프라에 직접적인 타격을 줄 가능성이 매우 높음.

📈 세션 누적: 14,954 / 1,000,000 (1.5%) | 세션 잔여 추정 985,046개

news_articles.title: 클로봇-에이프로, 사족보행 로봇과 AI 융합한 산업현장 자율 안전점검 사업 추진

news_articles.title: 사물인터넷혁신융합대학사업단, 디토닉 등 딥테크 기업과 사물인터넷 인재 양성 협약 체결

news_articles.title: 2026년 6월 다크웹 위협 행위자 동향 보고서
news_articles.summary: 알림 2026년 6월 다크웹 위협 행위자 동향 보고서는 핵티비스트를 포함해 딥웹 및 다크웹에서 활동하는 위협 행위자의 동향을 중심으로 작성되었다. 일부 내용은 사실 관계를 확인할 수 없다고 명시되었다. 주요 이슈 말레이시아에서는 지역 개발기관 및 보건 분야 공공 웹사이트를 대상으로 한 연쇄 변조·침해 사례가 확인되었다. 또한 같은 지역에서 중국 연계 APT 그룹으로 알려진 위협 행위자의 정부기관 대상 […]

news_articles.title: 2026년 6월 다크웹 이슈 동향 보고서
news_articles.summary: 알림 2026년 6월 다크웹 이슈 동향 보고서는 딥웹 및 다크웹에서 발생한 주요 이슈를 정리한 내용이다. 출처 특성상 일부 정보는 사실 여부를 완전히 확인하기 어려운 경우가 있어 이를 명시한다. 주요 이슈 Hasan의 BreachForums에서는 기존 운영자 diencracked의 복귀, HasanBroker와의 내부 갈등 공개, diencracked의 은퇴 선언, 그리고 L에게의 소유권 승계 정황이 이어졌다. 이로 인해 포럼의 지배 구조 안정성에 […]

news_articles.title: 2026년 6월 다크웹 침해사고 동향 보고서
news_articles.summary: 알림 2026년 6월 다크웹 침해사고 동향 보고서는 딥웹 및 다크웹 포럼에 게시된 주요 Data Breach(데이터 유출) 사례를 중심으로 구성되었다. 일부 정보는 출처 특성상 사실 여부를 완전히 확인하기 어려운 경우가 있어 검증이 필요한 내용이 포함되었다. 주요 이슈 ShinyHunters는 북미와 유럽 지역의 의료, 보안, 패션, 미디어, 유통 및 국제기구 등 다양한 분야의 기업·기관을 대상으로 연속적인 데이터 유출을 […]
